title: Spin correlations, low-energy scales, and anisotropy scaling in kagome frustrated magnets
authors: Phillip Popp, Stephan Rosenkranz, Arthur P. Ramirez, Sergey Syzranov
abstract: Neutron scattering is central to identifying quantum states of magnetic materials. In the search for quantum spin liquids, broad spectral features of inelastic spectra have been cited as evidence for spinon excitations, but can also arise from magnon excitations excitations in the presence of quenched disorder and strong magnon interactions. We develop a new approach to this problem, based on the adiabatic continuity in the $XXZ$ Heisenberg model on geometrically frustrating (GF) lattices as a function of the model's anisotropy. Using this approach, we identify universal features and energies of finite-temperature spin correlators. Focusing on the kagome lattice, we show that the low-energy spin spectral function contains robust, momentum-independent peaks with frequencies: $ω_1 \approx 3.4 T^*$ and $ω_2 \approx 6.3 T^*$, where the ``hidden energy scale'' $T^*$ is the characteristic scale of a low-temperature peak in the heat capacity, at which many GF magnets also display spin-glass freezing. We show that the spectral features at low energies $ω\lesssim T^*$ arise from single-magnon scattering and identify the magnetizations of the respective excitations. We explore the evolution of the spectral features with temperature and discuss extensions to other GF lattices. Our results provide a sharp spectroscopic criterion for interpreting neutron scattering in kagome and other GF quantum magnets.
summary: The paper analyzes the magnetic excitations in kagome antiferromagnets using the XXZ model and numerical simulations. By tracking spectral features across varying anisotropies, the authors establish that low-energy peaks scale universally with a characteristic energy scale $T^*$. This provides a sharp spectroscopic criterion to distinguish between different types of quantum magnetic excitations observed experimentally.
main_problem: The central problem is distinguishing between fractionalized excitations (spinons) and magnon excitations when interpreting broad spectral features observed in neutron scattering experiments on quantum magnetic materials.
main_result: The low-energy spin spectral function on the kagome lattice exhibits two robust, momentum-independent peaks ($\omega_1 \approx 3.4 T^*$ and $\omega_2 \approx 6.3 T^*$), suggesting these features arise from single-magnon scattering rather than a continuum of fractionalized excitations.
method: The authors use adiabatic continuity by tuning the anisotropy parameter ($\alpha$) in the XXZ Heisenberg model on geometrically frustrated lattices, simulating the dynamical spin structure factor using advanced numerical techniques like LTLM.
model_or_system: The system is the $S=1/2$ antiferromagnet on the kagome lattice, modeled by the anisotropic XXZ Heisenberg Hamiltonian. The analysis focuses on how spectral features evolve as a function of anisotropy and temperature.
key_observables: Dynamical spin structure factor ($	ilde{S}(\omega, T)$), low-energy excitation frequencies ($\omega_1, \omega_2$), and the 'hidden energy scale' ($T^*$) derived from heat capacity measurements.
important_parameters: Anisotropy parameter $\alpha$, temperature $T$, and the characteristic energy scale $T^*$ (related to spin-glass freezing).
main_assumptions: The low-energy excitations are assumed to be well-described by single-magnon transitions between specific magnetization bands ($|M_z|=0 	o 1$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the temperature and anisotropy dependence of $	ilde{S}(\omega, T)$, showing the robust scaling of peak energies ($\omega_1, \omega_2$) relative to $T^*$, alongside heat capacity data confirming $T^*$.
paper_structure: The paper develops a theoretical framework using adiabatic continuity on the XXZ model. It calculates $	ilde{S}(\omega, T)$ and demonstrates that low-energy spectral features scale universally with the characteristic temperature $T^*$, providing a spectroscopic criterion for interpreting neutron scattering data.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, quantitative method (the scaling law $\omega \propto T^*$) to interpret complex experimental signatures in frustrated magnets, bridging advanced quantum simulation techniques with measurable physical observables like inelastic neutron scattering spectra.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The transverse dynamical spin structure factor ˜S(ω, T) for various ratios α of the transverse to longitudinal exchange couplings and temperatures. Inset shows the structure factor at higher frequency for α = 0.05. Dashed black (lime) lines indicate the position of ω1(2)(α). (b) Dependence of the three energy scales T ∗, ω1, and ω2 on α (cf. panel c). Open symbols denote fits to ω1(2)(α) = A1(2)T ∗(α). (c) Heat capacity per spin of 24-site XXZ kagome clusters at the indicated values of α. The position of the low-temperature peak is denoted T ∗(α).
FIG. 2. Three possible energies of flipping a single spin in a ground state of the kagome-lattice Ising model. Filled (open) circles represent spin-up (down) states.

FIG. 3. The dynamical spin structure factor ˜S(ω, T = J/13) in the isotropic Heisenberg model (α = 1), in the three lowest- magnetization transition channels. Dashed black (green) line indicates the position of ω1(2).
FIG. 5. Temperature dependence of the areas R

FIG. 4. Low-energy |Mz| = 0 and |Mz| = 1 densities of states for an α = 1 cluster. Dashed lines indicate the positions of peaks that are relevant for the DSSF; arrows indicate the associated transition frequencies.
